bullets.text: Personal auto loss costs rose nearly 18% year over year, driven by used car prices up ~35% and broad inflation, pressuring collision, property damage, and total loss payouts throughout the quarter.

Progressive raised personal auto rates in 36 states representing 75% of trailing 12-month premium, an average ~7% increase—one of the highest quarterly rate actions ever—following an 8% full-year increase in 2021, with ~7 points still to earn in.

Companywide combined ratio was 94.5 for Q1 2022, up 5.2 points year over year; Personal Lines CR was 95.2 and Property CR was 98.5 including 17.8 points of catastrophe losses from March wind and hail events.

Companywide net premiums written grew 12% in Q1 2022, with Commercial Lines up 63%, Property up 13%, and Personal Lines up 3%, reflecting strong commercial momentum but personal auto rate-driven moderation.
Companywide PIF growth slowed to 3% year over year as higher personal auto rates drove a significant decline in new business applications compared to the stimulus-boosted Q1 2021.

Progressive issued $1.5 billion of senior unsecured notes in March across 5-, 10-, and 30-year maturities to rebuild capital cushion and return leverage to historical range after entering 2022 at a below-normal level.

Commercial Lines crossed $9 billion in trailing 12-month NPW in March 2022, reaching nearly 1 million policies in force; BOP expanded to 36 states and heavy truck roadside assistance launched in seven states.

Personal auto product model 8.7 was deployed in 27 states representing 60% of countrywide premium, with early results showing improved quote-to-customer conversion, especially for multicar and prior-insurance segments.

Wind, hail, and tornadoes across Texas and the Southeast drove 17.8 catastrophe loss ratio points in Property for Q1 2022; the personal auto impact was more modest but contributed to the above-target combined ratio.
